quote: The base retains its coda consonant k unchanged; the h is the onset of the grammaticalized suffix hi 'thing, place'. suggestion: Update the reference to '§2 below' to accurately reflect the section number where 'sis' is discussed, or remove the specific section reference if it's clear from context.

quote:
• (a) Length-first (Hattori 1967; De Boer 2010, both via ): Proto-Ainu had contrastive vowel length and no pitch accent; Hokkaido acquired its pitch system through prolonged contact with Japanese, while Sakhalin preserved the older length contrast.
  • (b) Both length and pitch (Vovin 1993, via ; ): Proto-Ainu carried both phonemic length and pitch simultaneously; the Sakhalin branch lost pitch under Amur–Sakhalin contact pressure, retaining length; Hokkaido retained pitch and neutralised length.
  • (c) Pitch-first (Satō 2015–2016, via ): Proto-Ainu had pitch accent only; ‹SA› length is a phonetically conditioned phenomenon restricted to open syllables and represents a Sakhalin-internal development — possibly compensatory lengthening following coda weakening — rather than an inherited contrast.
issue: The descriptions of views (a) and (b) present conflicting claims about whether Proto-Ainu had length, pitch, or both. View (a) states Proto-Ainu had length but no pitch, while view (b) states Proto-Ainu had both length and pitch. These are mutually exclusive claims about the proto-language's prosodic system.
suggestion: Clarify the precise claims of each view regarding Proto-Ainu prosody, ensuring they are distinct and accurately represented.

quote:
Alonso de la Fuente's own treatment is internally divided: §2.3 endorses view (c) as "most
economical", while §2.4 states that "Hattori was right when he suggested Proto-Ainu had only
vowel length, and no pitch accent; pitch accent may be the result of language contact after
Japanese influence" — a restatement of view (a)
<Ref k="alonso2022" p="§2.3, §2.4" /> ‹contested›. The two sections advocate opposite
positions without reconciliation; neither can be taken as Alonso's settled conclusion.
issue: The text explicitly states that Alonso de la Fuente's work presents contradictory views on the proto-Ainu prosodic system in different sections (§2.3 and §2.4), endorsing both view (c) and view (a) without reconciliation. This is a direct internal contradiction within the author's own analysis as presented in the chapter.
suggestion: Either reconcile the two positions attributed to Alonso de la Fuente, or clearly present them as conflicting interpretations within his work, perhaps by noting the specific reasons for the divergence or stating that his conclusion is unsettled.

quote: ‹SA› noociw : HA nocíw, ‹SA› niisew : HA niséw (length ↔ low pitch in HA)

The parenthetical note '(length ↔ low pitch in HA)' seems to contradict the acute accent mark, which typically indicates high pitch. This creates confusion about the pitch representation in the Hokkaido forms.
suggestion: Clarify the meaning of the acute accent in the Hokkaido forms, or correct the parenthetical note to accurately reflect the relationship between length and pitch.
